speaker: interviewer
text: '96-- we're speaking with Sol Einhorn. Sol, before we meet your lovely wife, Beverly, and talk to her for a few minutes, I just wanted to ask you one more thing. Do you ever dream about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: There isn't a night, I shouldn't think of and dream about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that the Holocaust affected the way you deal with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't think so. I don't believe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you more protective of them than you think other American parents might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your attitude about food and wasting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that, I can't stand, to waste food. Yeah, once a person's hungry in life, and I always like to see a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- when I see food, any time, I always eat more than I supposed to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't want to waste anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- not only wasting, I always remind myself from the hunger. [INAUDIBLE] have food, what hunger means. Always comes to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. All right, now, Beverly? We're going to speak to Beverly. As the wife of a Holocaust survivor-- of course, you can't compare, never having been married to somebody else. But did you find there were certain sensitivities you had to either develop or that you had to put into play in your relationship with Sol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course there were. There were times when he would get very enraged. And at that time, I knew that we had to either calm him down or just be quiet till he got over the enragement. Very often, it happened on a Shabbat meal when the children were sitting around the table. And I just did the best I could to keep them calm. And I know-- I understood where it was coming from.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really terrific. When you were young-- now, you were a born, bred American.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, I always felt very lucky that my grandparents came over to America in 1900. Otherwise, we probably would have been killed or shot, as the rest of the people were who lived around Kiev, in the Ukraine, in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your life wasn't involved, like with an immigrant society or an immigrant population.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all-- parents were brought up in America. They came as young children. And they were-- their main idea was to Americanize.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were there a lot of adjustments you had to make in your relationship with Sol, because he had such a totally different background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really-- we thought alike in a lot of things. And there's always that Jewish background that keeps you together. And even though I didn't come from a religious background and he came from a very religious background, our parents gave us a very strong feeling for Judaism. And it came out through the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message that you would like to give, having lived with a person like Sol and known what of his experiences were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, really, what to say. I think that the World War II is responsible for a lot of things that are happening in the world today. And I wonder if we couldn't learn from this not to hate each other, and to understand somebody who is different from us, and to teach our children tolerance. If we had more tolerance, maybe so many-- maybe so many people would not have died or been killed, as they were, and suffered as much as they did in the concentration camps, in the prison camps, and even in the battlefields of the World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my grandfather, Yaakov Einhorn--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: His [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one? The one on the right or the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, this is-- this is Zalman Hirschtic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he was about 65 years of age, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the one on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This other, [NON-ENGLISH] Einhorn, my grandfather. This is the son of [NON-ENGLISH] Einhorn, [NON-ENGLISH] was riding the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] Auschwitz, about 60, 62 years of marriage. [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a Yeshiva right after the war, the 1945, where they organized small Yeshiva age of 15 to 20. And I am on the corner of the table there, and sitting-- matter of fact, I remember Gomorrah we learned [NON-ENGLISH]. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the Yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're where, on the left of the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side, on the corner. That's 55 years, 52 years ago. This is a picture [INAUDIBLE] America, when I arrived in 1946 with Ahava, in front of the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, the double-breasted jacket with the black hat or the blue hat. This is a picture that was taken in Sweden, right after the war in 1945. A lot of people tell me that I have grown, grown a hat since this picture. When I came to the United States, in the United States I grow a whole hat. Thank you. This is a picture for me, but it was taken here. It was taken in the United States, only about two years after I arrived to the United States. It shows that I grew, like they say, a hat taller. This is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it, Sol?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947, '48. This is my brother-- or the only brother what remained alive from nine children, but [NON-ENGLISH] with father and mother. In But he got married in the United States in 1954-- him, and his wife, and me. All right, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the left-hand--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one with the glasses?

speaker: interviewee
text: -- Right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one with the glasses? What's your brother's--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the glasses, with the glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Itzik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itzik Einhorn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] This is my son-in-law, Steve Garber, and my daughter, Marilyn Garber-- lovely couple, and they have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken? Where are they living?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in-- in Queens. This is Steve and Marilyn, my son-in-law and my daughter, and my three grandchildren, Jeffrey, Ryan, and Megan. This is Benny and Ruthie Esquinazi, my son-in-law and my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken about six years, seven years ago. This is the Esquinazis' children, my daughter and son-in-law. Their names are Yosef, Moshe, and Estee, ages six, four, and two. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Shh. This is my daughter Mindy and Phil Dauber.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken a couple of years ago, two years ago. They live in Manhattan. They're a very happy couple, and they have a lovely daughter by the name--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the baby's name? What's the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amanda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amanda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll show Amanda in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely granddaughter Amanda, Phil and Mindy's beautiful daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She'll be about a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on this picture, she's what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The picture of my three lovely daughters, Marilyn, Ruthie, and Mindy. This photo was us taken at the marriage of Marilyn. That's 12 years ago. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two on the side are twins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my grandchildren on Hanukkah, a Hanukkah evening, when I'm giving them out Hanukkah gelt. They used to put them up on the line. And there there's a friend of ours, also, there in the line, a grown-up. All right, thank you, and happy Hanukkah. [LAUGHS]
